Name: jacobian_guided_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping: Training-Free Latency Optimization via Local Sensitivity Analysis
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic layer skipping guided by local Jacobian sensitivity can minimize hidden-state perturbation while enforcing strict latency budgets, outperforming heuristic layer-skipping methods in latency-critical scenarios.
Related Work: Existing layer-skipping methods, such as Sortblock and Spectral Quantization Routing, rely on fixed heuristics or activation-based proxies, failing to adapt to dynamic input distributions or enforce rigorous latency guarantees. JGLS distinguishes itself by leveraging local Jacobian sensitivity to make principled layer-skipping decisions, grounded in optimization theory and explicitly bounded by wall-clock latency.
Abstract: Latency-critical inference demands efficient computation without sacrificing accuracy. We propose Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping (JGLS), a training-free framework that dynamically skips layers based on the local Jacobian sensitivity of hidden states. By computing Jacobian norms on-the-fly using randomized matrix-free methods, JGLS identifies layers whose skipping minimally perturbs the optimization landscape. We theoretically ground JGLS in PAC-Bayes bounds, linking Jacobian sensitivity to generalization stability under layer-skipping noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, JGLS achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, JGLS provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.

Experiments.Description: Compare JGLS against heuristic layer-skipping baselines (e.g., fixed skipping ratios, activation-based proxies) on WILDS benchmarks.
Experiments.Method: Implement JGLS with randomized matrix-free Jacobian computation and evaluate its performance under strict latency budgets.

Experiments.Description: Validate the robustness of JGLS under domain shifts by testing on out-of-distribution datasets.
Experiments.Method: Apply JGLS to WILDS benchmarks with domain shifts and measure its performance compared to baselines.

Experiments.Description: Analyze the trade-off between Jacobian computation overhead and layer-skipping efficiency.
Experiments.Method: Vary the frequency of Jacobian computation and measure its impact on overall latency and accuracy.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Jacobian computation overhead may outweigh the benefits of layer skipping in some scenarios.
The framework assumes a well-conditioned optimization landscape, which may not hold for all models or tasks.
Dynamic layer skipping may introduce non-deterministic behavior, complicating deployment in latency-critical systems.
